papers.title: Enhancing protein expression in humans through codon optimization with transformer and contrastive learning.
papers.abstract: Following the success of COVID-19 vaccines and therapies, mRNA-based therapeutics have attracted significant attention. Codon optimization is crucial for improving translation efficiency and mRNA stability, both of which are necessary for developing effective mRNA vaccines and drugs. Traditional approaches often rely on codon usage tables and other frequency-based heuristics, which neglect sequence context and show limited scalability. We introduce COformer, a deep learning framework for codon optimization that captures codon-level features related to protein expression in Homo sapiens cells.
papers.summary: This paper introduces COformer, a deep learning model that uses transformer and contrastive learning to optimize codons for enhanced protein expression in human cells, outperforming traditional frequency-based methods. The approach captures sequence context to improve translation efficiency and mRNA stability, which is critical for developing effective mRNA vaccines and therapies.

papers.title: An optimized dual-branch method for DNA enhancer identification based on pretrained models and multi-scale local regulatory motif extraction.
papers.abstract: Accurate identification of enhancer sequences requires concurrent modeling of global contextual information and local regulatory motifs in DNA. Although the pretrained language model DNABERT-2 can effectively capture long-range dependencies, it is still limited in representing position-sensitive local patterns such as transcription factor binding sites. To address this issue, we propose iEnhancer-Hybrid, a model with a dual branch architecture. One branch leverages DNABERT-2 to extract global contextual features from the input sequence, whereas the other branch employs a three-layer multi scal
papers.summary: This paper introduces iEnhancer-Hybrid, a computational method that combines a pretrained DNA language model (DNABERT-2) for global sequence context with a multi-scale convolutional branch for local regulatory motif extraction, improving the accuracy of enhancer DNA sequence identification. The dual-branch architecture addresses the limitation of pretrained models in capturing position-sensitive local patterns like transcription factor binding sites.

papers.title: Deep learning enables rabies virus detection and antibody quantification in cell culture.
papers.abstract: Rabies remains a major neglected disease, causing tens of thousands of deaths annually in endemic regions. Cell culture-based assays are central to rabies diagnostics and antibody quantification, yet interpretation relies on manual fluorescence image reading, which is labour-intensive, subjective, and difficult to scale. Here, we present, what is to our knowledge, the first AI-based workflow for detecting rabies virus infection in BHK-21 cell cultures and quantifying antibody titres. Three state-of-the-art architectures-ResNet, Swin Transformer V2, and MambaOut-were trained on a large dataset
papers.summary: This study introduces the first AI-based workflow for detecting rabies virus and quantifying antibodies in cell cultures, using deep learning architectures like ResNet, Swin Transformer V2, and MambaOut to automate fluorescence image analysis. The approach reduces manual labor and subjectivity, potentially improving scalability and accuracy in rabies diagnostics and antibody quantification.

papers.title: Quality Evaluation of Large Language Model-Assisted Generation of Initial Senior Physician Ward Round Records for Patients With Acute Poisoning: Cross-Sectional Study.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown potential in medical text generation. Senior physician ward round records are critical documents whose quality reflects the accuracy and continuity of clinical decision-making. The initial record is particularly important, as it represents the first formal senior-level synthesis of a patient's presentation, establishing the diagnostic framework and treatment direction for all subsequent care. The quality of LLM-generated initial records for acute poisoning remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study evaluates the quality of initial senior physician ward round records generated by large language models (LLMs) for patients with acute poisoning. The findings reveal that while LLMs show potential for medical text generation, the quality of their generated records for this specific clinical context remains uncertain. This work highlights the need for further refinement before LLMs can reliably assist in generating critical clinical documentation.

papers.title: Enhancing Cross-Context Generalization in Drug Perturbation Prediction with a Multimodal Conditional Diffusion Framework.
papers.abstract: Predicting drug-induced transcriptional perturbations is critical for precision medicine, yet existing models fail to capture multimodal biological context, limiting generalization across unseen drugs and cell lines.
papers.summary: This paper introduces a multimodal conditional diffusion framework that improves prediction of drug-induced gene expression changes across different cell types and drugs. By integrating multiple biological data types, the model achieves better generalization to unseen drug-cell line combinations than existing methods.

papers.title: SPEAK: Spatial Prompting with Expert Aligned Knowledge for Tissue Domain Identification in Spatial Transcriptomics.
papers.abstract: Spatially resolved transcriptomic (SRT) data requires spatial domain identification to enable tissue microenvironment-specific downstream analyses. Here we present SPEAK (Spatial Prompting with Expert-Aligned Knowledge), a large language model (LLM)-based method to identify spatial domains from SRT data by taking advantage of the prior knowledge from both LLM and human experts. SPEAK constructs a spatial context prompt for each cell/spot based on cell types and marker genes of its neighboring cells, enabling zero-shot inference, expert-guided fine-tuning, and prototype updating through two-sta
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: SPEAK is a new method that uses large language models (LLMs) and expert knowledge to identify spatial domains in spatial transcriptomics data. It constructs spatial context prompts for each cell based on its neighbors' cell types and marker genes, enabling zero-shot inference and expert-guided fine-tuning.

papers.title: NanoCellAnnotator: Formalizing Expert Cell Type Annotation with Large Language Models.
papers.abstract: Cell-type annotation in spatial transcriptomics is challenging due to sparse gene panels, spatial heterogeneity, and limited availability of tissue-matched reference atlases. Recent approaches have explored large language models (LLMs) for integrating biological knowledge during annotation, but unconstrained inference can produce biologically unsupported predictions and hallucinated cell types. In addition, many LLM-based pipelines rely on large cloud-hosted models that limit reproducibility and deployment in privacy-sensitive environments.
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: NanoCellAnnotator uses large language models to improve cell type annotation in spatial transcriptomics by constraining predictions to biologically supported cell types, reducing hallucinations. It is designed to run locally, addressing reproducibility and privacy concerns associated with cloud-hosted models.

papers.title: trAIt: Species-by-Trait Data Retrieval using Large Language Models.
papers.abstract: Biological research often requires information about species' traits. Manual literature collation can be time-consuming and miss parts of the literature. To address this gap, we developed trAIt , a publicly available software for the retrieval of characteristics of species from scientific literature catalogued in the Europe PubMed Central (PubMed) database. trAIt provides a graphical user interface (GUI) in which users specify species and characteristics of interest. Leveraging a large language model (LLM), trAIt retrieves relevant papers, combines their content through a consensus-based summa
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: trAIt is a new software tool that uses a large language model to automatically retrieve species trait information from the scientific literature, saving researchers time compared to manual literature searches. It provides a graphical user interface where users can specify species and traits of interest, and it aggregates information from multiple papers using a consensus-based approach. This tool addresses a common bottleneck in biological research by making trait data extraction more efficient and comprehensive.

papers.title: HCGT-PL: a heterogeneous contrastive graph transformer unifying protein-ligand affinity prediction and structure-based virtual screening.
papers.abstract: Structure-based virtual screening and binding affinity prediction remain challenging due to solvation/entropy effects, protein flexibility, and induced fit. We present a Heterogeneous Contrastive Graph Transformer for Protein-Ligand (HCGT-PL) framework. Each complex is represented as a directed heterogeneous graph with multiple node and relation types; relation-specific multi-head attention enables message passing and aggregation. Unsupervised augmentations yield transferable interaction representations that are fine-tuned for affinity regression and virtual screening. Across diverse benchmark
papers.summary: The authors developed a new deep learning framework called HCGT-PL that represents protein-ligand complexes as directed heterogeneous graphs, using relation-specific attention to better capture molecular interactions. The model learns transferable interaction representations through unsupervised augmentation, then fine-tunes for both binding affinity prediction and virtual screening tasks, showing improved performance across diverse benchmarks.

papers.title: Deep Learning Predicts Dissimilar DNA-DNA Binding and Engineers Hyperconnected Networks.
papers.abstract: Common frameworks in molecular bioengineering and synthetic biology focus on orthogonality, viewing weak or non-specific interactions as problems to avoid. This constrains the usable sequence space, limits scalability, and neglects scenarios where synthetic systems must operate within natural backgrounds of high sequence diversity. Harnessing the full space is difficult because models are lacking that can accurately and quickly predict non-orthogonal interactions and be validated against ground truth data. Here we develop BINND - Binding and Interaction Neural Network for DNA - using DNA-DNA i
papers.summary: This paper introduces BINND, a deep learning model that predicts non-orthogonal DNA-DNA binding interactions, enabling the design of hyperconnected networks rather than avoiding weak or cross-reactive binding. By harnessing the full sequence space, the approach expands scalability and functionality for synthetic biology applications operating in diverse genetic backgrounds.

papers.title: A new protein panel for the diagnosis of HFpEF: combining machine-learning and liquid-chromatography mass-spectrometry proteomics.
papers.abstract: The diagnosis of heart failure with a preserved left ventricular ejection fraction (HFpEF) remains challenging. Plasma concentrations of NT-proBNP can rule out HFpEF with good sensitivity, but only moderate specificity. Therefore, there is a high unmet need for better diagnostic biomarkers with improved specificity.
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning combined with liquid-chromatography mass-spectrometry proteomics to identify a new panel of protein biomarkers that may improve the diagnosis of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), a condition that is currently difficult to diagnose accurately. The new panel aims to provide better specificity than the current standard biomarker, NT-proBNP, which has limited specificity.

papers.title: Mind the gap: An embedding guide to safely travel in sequence space.
papers.abstract: We present a hybrid approach combining a protein language model (pLM) with Monte Carlo (MC) sampling for generating enzyme mutants free of mutations deleterious for structural preservation. Given the amino acid sequence of the original enzyme and a set of residues for which the local environment should be conserved, i.e., the catalytic site, our approach generates mutants that differ vastly in the overall sequence while retaining the geometry of the conserved region, thereby representing promising candidates for further experimental screening. Unlike end-to-end deep-learning approaches, whose
papers.summary: This paper introduces a hybrid method that combines a protein language model with Monte Carlo sampling to generate enzyme mutants that preserve the geometry of key functional regions (like catalytic sites) while allowing large changes elsewhere in the sequence. This approach offers a more targeted and structurally-aware alternative to end-to-end deep learning for designing novel enzyme variants for experimental screening.

papers.title: Association and predictive value of preoperative high-sensitivity C-reactive protein for postoperative atrial fibrillation after video-assisted thoracoscopic lobectomy: a cohort study using the INSPIRE database.
papers.abstract: Postoperative atrial fibrillation (POAF) is a common and serious complication following video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS), which affects patients' medical outcomes and quality of life. The predictive value of high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) as an inflammatory marker for POAF in non-cardiac surgeries remains unclear. This study aims to investigate the association and predictive capability of preoperative hs-CRP levels with postoperative new-onset atrial fibrillation.
papers.summary: This study found that higher preoperative levels of high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP), an inflammatory marker, are associated with an increased risk of developing atrial fibrillation after video-assisted thoracoscopic lung surgery. Using data from the INSPIRE database, the researchers suggest that hs-CRP levels could help predict this common postoperative complication. The findings indicate that inflammation plays a role in postoperative atrial fibrillation and that measuring hs-CRP before surgery may aid in risk assessment.

papers.title: A unified vision-language model for precision oncology and biomarker prediction in neuroblastoma.
papers.abstract: Neuroblastoma is a leading cause of childhood cancer mortality, presenting persistent management challenges due to its biological heterogeneity and the limited accessibility of molecular profiling in routine practice. Here we present NEVA (NEuroblastoma Vision-language AI), a multimodal foundation model designed to address these barriers. Unlike conventional approaches that rely on frozen encoders and multiple instance learning, NEVA implements a pathologist-inspired hierarchical workflow with end-to-end optimization. Developed and evaluated in a large multi-institutional cohort of 1,238 patie
papers.summary: Researchers developed NEVA, a multimodal AI model that analyzes both pathology images and clinical text to predict biomarkers and outcomes in neuroblastoma, a childhood cancer. Unlike previous methods, NEVA uses a hierarchical approach inspired by how pathologists examine tissue, and was trained on over 1,200 patient cases from multiple institutions. This model could make precision oncology more accessible by reducing reliance on costly molecular profiling.

papers.title: Decoding cancer circulating transcriptomic signatures with language models.
papers.abstract: Current liquid biopsy methods for multi-cancer detection using plasma cell-free RNA (cfRNA, short RNA fragments circulating in blood that can reflect disease states) typically rely on gene annotations, which can overlook signals from unannotated or repetitive genomic regions. We present GeneLLM, a Transformer-based model that directly processes the nucleotide sequences of human-mapped cfRNA reads to identify cancer-indicative signatures. By bypassing gene-level quantification, the model retains signals from transcriptomic dark matter. The model learns latent pseudo-biomarkers (prototype repres
papers.summary: GeneLLM is a new AI model that analyzes the raw genetic sequences of RNA fragments in blood to detect cancer, bypassing the need for pre-defined gene annotations. This approach captures signals from previously ignored genomic 'dark matter', potentially improving multi-cancer detection from a simple blood test.

papers.title: Integrative profiling of senescence-associated cells and genes identifies UGT1A7 as a prognostic and therapeutic target in Lung squamous cell carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Lung Squamous Cell Carcinoma (LUSC), a common subtype of non-small cell lung cancer, primarily occurs in elderly individuals and is closely linked to senescence-related biological processes. Meanwhile, senescence reshapes the tumor microenvironment by promoting immune suppression and angiogenesis, and its underlying mechanisms remain unclear. Here, we identified three senescence-related molecular subtypes of LUSC, each with distinct clinical outcomes, genomic alterations, and immune features. Among them, Cluster 1 was associated with poor prognosis, increased genomic instability, and an immuno
papers.summary: This study identifies three distinct senescence-related molecular subtypes of lung squamous cell carcinoma (LUSC) with different clinical outcomes and immune features. The gene UGT1A7 is highlighted as a potential prognostic marker and therapeutic target, particularly in a poor-prognosis subtype characterized by genomic instability and immune suppression.

papers.title: Predicting 3-year mortality after myocardial infarction in a structured care program: A comparative analysis of Cox regression and machine learning models.
papers.abstract: Accurate risk stratification after myocardial infarction (MI) remains essential for optimizing long-term management. The advantage of machine learning (ML) over conventional statistical models in real-world clinical settings remains uncertain.
papers.summary: This study compared machine learning models with traditional Cox regression for predicting 3-year mortality after myocardial infarction in a structured care program. The researchers found that machine learning did not significantly outperform the conventional statistical model, suggesting that simpler methods may be sufficient for this clinical prediction task.

papers.title: Machine learning reveals mechanisms and feedstock effects on potassium and magnesium uptake by wheat in biochar-amended soil in greenhouse.
papers.abstract: Despite the potential of biochar to enhance crop nutrient uptake, the complex, feedstock-dependent interactions among biochar properties, soil biological processes, and plant root traits remain poorly understood. Hence, this study employed machine learning algorithms (Linear regression and Random Forest) with advanced feature selection methods (Correlation-based Feature Subset Selection Evaluator, Correlation-Based Attribute Evaluator, Principal Components Attribute Transformer, and Stepwise Regression) to predict potassium (K+) and magnesium (Mg2+) uptake by wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) roots
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning to predict how different biochar feedstocks affect potassium and magnesium uptake in wheat roots, revealing that feedstock type significantly influences nutrient availability through changes in soil properties and root traits. The findings help clarify the complex interactions between biochar, soil biology, and plant nutrition, offering a data-driven approach to optimize biochar use in agriculture.

papers.title: GenART: Reading the genome's language with adaptive "words".
papers.abstract: Tokenization is both a prerequisite and a central challenge for genomic language models, due to the inherent difficulty of delineating meaningful segments within continuous DNA sequences. We present GenART, a genomic language model framework that dynamically segments DNA into variable-length words directly from raw sequences without manual annotation. Its key adaptive tokenization module infers biologically meaningful boundaries from multi-scale contextual signals during pretraining. GenART demonstrates competitive performance across diverse tasks, outperforming leading approaches. Word bounda
papers.summary: GenART is a new genomic language model that learns to automatically segment DNA sequences into variable-length 'words' without needing manual annotation, unlike previous methods that rely on fixed tokenization. By dynamically identifying biologically meaningful boundaries during training, it outperforms leading approaches on a variety of genomic tasks. This adaptive tokenization method could improve how AI models understand the functional grammar of the genome.

papers.title: Phylogenetic analysis and machine learning identify signatures of selection and predict deleterious mutations in common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris L.).
papers.abstract: Mutations are a continuous source of new alleles and genetic diversity in populations. Domestication and selection influence the accumulation of alleles occurring across a range of deleteriousness. Evidence suggests that mildly deleterious mutations (DelMut) can be purged out of breeding populations, increasing favorable allele accumulation.
papers.summary: This study combines phylogenetic analysis and machine learning to identify signatures of selection and predict deleterious mutations in common bean. The findings suggest that domestication and selection can purge mildly deleterious mutations, thereby increasing the accumulation of favorable alleles in breeding populations.

papers.title: A comprehensive dataset of 32 million pentapeptide structures for high-throughput virtual screening.
papers.abstract: Small peptides are widely used as binders, modulators, and structural motifs, but their conformational flexibility complicates structure-based analysis and high-throughput screening. We present an open dataset of three-dimensional structures for the complete space of canonical amino-acid pentapeptides: 3,200,000 unique sequences with up to 10 conformers per sequence, for a total of 32,000,000 peptide conformers. Structures were generated directly from sequence using an automated workflow built on UCSF ChimeraX for model construction, Reduce for hydrogen placement, and RDKit for conformer gener
papers.summary: Researchers generated an open dataset of 32 million 3D structures covering all possible pentapeptide sequences (3.2 million unique sequences, each with up to 10 conformers). This resource enables high-throughput virtual screening and structure-based analysis of small peptides without requiring experimental structures.

papers.title: AI/ML-enabled multi-omics integration of host genetics, immunity, and the gut microbiome in Crohn's disease: From diagnosis to theranostics.
papers.abstract: Crohn's disease is a long-term inflammatory disorder arising from the interaction of genetic risk factors, immune system dysfunction, and alterations in gut microbiota. Variability in clinical phenotypes and lack of biomarker specificity hinder the efficiency of current traditional diagnostic and treatment approaches. This review aims to assess how AI- and ML-driven multi-omics offer comprehensive insights into pathogenicity, thereby enhancing diagnostic techniques and personalized therapeutic approaches in CD. Current studies employ integration of multi-omics like genomics, proteomics, transc
papers.summary: This review examines how AI and machine learning can integrate multi-omics data—including host genetics, immunity, and gut microbiome—to improve diagnosis and personalized treatment for Crohn's disease. By combining genomics, proteomics, and other omics layers, these approaches offer deeper insights into disease mechanisms and biomarker discovery.

papers.title: Evolution of CTCF binding sites in the human genome.
papers.abstract: The CCCTC-binding factor (CTCF) is a master regulator of topologically associating domains (TADs), which shape 3D genome architecture and gene regulation. While TAD evolution across species has been extensively studied, the evolution of CTCF binding sites (CBSs) in primates remains under-explored. Here, we employed a deep learning model to predict genome-wide CBSs in five primate species, including human, chimpanzee, gorilla, orangutan, and macaque. We found that approximately half of human CBSs are conserved across all five species. These primate-conserved CBSs cluster near TAD boundaries, ex
papers.summary: This study uses a deep learning model to predict CTCF binding sites across five primate species, finding that about half of human sites are conserved. These conserved sites cluster near TAD boundaries, highlighting their role in 3D genome architecture evolution.

papers.title: Smiles-based bioactivity prediction through molecular encoder selection and data augmentation.
papers.abstract: Quantitative prediction of inhibitor potency can accelerate early-stage drug discovery. Recently, data-driven approaches have gained widespread interest in drug discovery, as evidenced by a growing number of benchmarking challenges and open competitions. In this context, we developed a machine learning-based methodology that can find the most effective way of predicting IC50 values against ASK1 from SMILES, for "Jump AI(.py) 2025: 3rd AI Drug Discovery Competition", hosted by the Korea Pharmaceutical and Bio-Pharma Manufacturers Association (KPBMA) on the Dacon platform. Applying our methodolo
papers.summary: This study presents a machine learning pipeline that selects the best molecular encoder and applies data augmentation to predict IC50 values of inhibitors against ASK1 from SMILES strings. The approach was developed for a drug discovery competition and demonstrates how combining encoder selection with augmentation can improve bioactivity prediction accuracy.

papers.title: A self-attention-based deep learning model for identifying key genes in insect pupal metamorphosis.
papers.abstract: Metamorphosis is the major innovation in the evolution of insect, playing an important role in environmental adaptation and biodiversity formation. However, it has been a challenge to identify the key genes in insect pupal metamorphosis. In this study, we constructed a library of word embeddings based on Word2vec for 293 insect protein sequences from 14 orders. A gene network classification model (GNCM) based on deep learning (DL) and self-attention mechanisms (SAM) was designed to identify key genes by calculating their importance (weights) in the metamorphosis of insect pupae. Empirical stud
papers.summary: This paper presents a deep learning model that uses self-attention mechanisms and word embeddings to identify key genes involved in insect pupal metamorphosis. The model calculates gene importance weights from a network built from 293 insect protein sequences across 14 orders.

papers.title: A population-based retrospective machine learning study of COVID-19 severity using integrated clinical and viral genomic data in Jiangsu Province, China.
papers.abstract: As coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) has transitioned into an endemic phase characterized by sustained transmission and widespread hybrid immunity, understanding region-specific determinants of severe disease remains important for real-world risk stratification and public health planning.
papers.summary: This study used machine learning to analyze clinical and viral genomic data from COVID-19 patients in Jiangsu Province, China, identifying key factors associated with severe disease. The findings help improve risk stratification and public health planning for COVID-19 in the endemic phase.

papers.title: Harnessing Large-Scale Multi-Omics Data for Risk Prediction and Deep Phenotyping of Valvular Heart Diseases in the General Population.
papers.abstract: The risk profile of valvular heart disease (VHD) and its underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study aimed to develop and validate a multi-omics-based risk prediction model, and to elucidate potential biological mechanisms. Using data from the UK Biobank, Cox proportional hazards and machine learning models (XGBoost and LightGBM) were evaluated for predicting VHD and its subtypes (aortic valve stenosis, AVS; aortic valve regurgitation, AVR; mitral valve regurgitation, MVR). Cox models based on key clinical factors showed the best predictive performance (C-index of 0.75-0.81), wh
papers.summary: This study uses UK Biobank data to develop multi-omics risk prediction models for valvular heart diseases (VHD), finding that clinical factor-based Cox models outperform machine learning approaches. The work also explores biological mechanisms underlying VHD subtypes, including aortic and mitral valve conditions.

papers.title: A multi-view feature fusion framework with interpretable graph convolution for predicting microbe-drug associations.
papers.abstract: Predicting associations between human microbes and drugs (MDA) is a critical step in drug development and precision medicine. Although various computational approaches have been proposed, many existing models still struggle to reveal the key features and interaction mechanisms that drive their predictions, and their decision processes remain difficult to interpret and validate biologically. To address these limitations, we propose IDEAL (Interpretability-Driven Evolvable Attentive Learning for Microbe-Drug Association), a multi-view framework that integrates drug network topological attributes
papers.summary: The authors introduce IDEAL, a new computational framework that uses graph convolution and multi-view feature fusion to predict interactions between human microbes and drugs. Unlike previous models, IDEAL is designed to be interpretable, helping researchers understand the key features and mechanisms driving its predictions. This could aid drug development and precision medicine by revealing how microbes influence drug responses.

papers.title: AI-guided CRISPR screening reveals therapeutic targets in psoriasis.
papers.abstract: Psoriasis affects over 125 million people globally. Biologics targeting the IL-17/IL-17RA axis are effective but require systemic administration, are costly, and are unsuitable for some patients. Developing topical small-molecule alternatives requires a better understanding of how IL-17 receptor A (IL17RA) is regulated in keratinocytes, the principal effector cells of psoriatic lesions. Here, we report a genome-wide CRISPR knockout screen for regulators of surface IL17RA in primary human epidermal keratinocytes. We prioritize hits using experimental enrichment together with VirtualCRISPR, a la
papers.summary: This study uses a genome-wide CRISPR screen combined with a machine learning tool called VirtualCRISPR to identify regulators of IL-17 receptor A in keratinocytes, uncovering new therapeutic targets for topical psoriasis treatments. The approach prioritizes hits that could lead to small-molecule alternatives to current systemic biologics.

papers.title: Accurate prediction of asparagine deamidation in biologics using advanced machine learning models.
papers.abstract: The spontaneous deamidation of asparagine residues remains a major obstacle to the stability and efficacy of protein therapeutics. Currently available models in the literature for predicting deamidation liabilities can suffer from limited generalizability, likely due to biases such as sequence similarity within datasets. In this study, we built machine learning models using protein language models (PLMs, e.g. ESM2) and graph neural networks (GNNs), trained on a comprehensive dataset of 591 asparagine sites from over 105 protein molecules. To address the critical issue of data leakage, we imple
papers.summary: Researchers developed machine learning models using protein language models and graph neural networks to predict asparagine deamidation in therapeutic proteins, training on a dataset of 591 asparagine sites from over 105 protein molecules. The models address data leakage issues and aim to improve generalizability over existing prediction tools, helping to identify stability liabilities in biologics.

papers.title: scGenoByte: a GenoByte embedding transformer with biological priors for cell type annotation.
papers.abstract: Effective cell representation learning is crucial for accurate cell annotation and the deciphering of cellular heterogeneity in single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) analysis. Current foundation models have achieved superior performance compared with traditional methods. However, due to data sparsity and the complexity of model, existing methods often compromise by selecting highly variable genes or filtering for nonzero expressions, which discard potentially significant genes. Thus, modeling the complete transcriptome for cell representation remains computationally challenging; we present sc
papers.summary: scGenoByte is a new transformer model that embeds the full transcriptome for cell type annotation from single-cell RNA-seq data, overcoming limitations of previous methods that discard potentially important genes due to data sparsity. By incorporating biological priors, it achieves accurate cell representation and annotation without filtering out highly variable or nonzero-expressed genes.

papers.title: EssTFNet: integration of adaptive time-frequency and DNA language models for interpretable human essential gene prediction.
papers.abstract: Essential genes are defined as indispensable for an organism's survival. The loss of function of these genes results in cell death or an inability to complete the normal life cycle. Research on essential genes is pivotal in elucidating the origin and evolution of life, as well as in identifying potential therapeutic targets. Therefore, predicting essential genes is of great scientific importance and has many applications in basic research and the biomedical field. In this study, we propose EssTFNet, a novel, interpretable deep learning framework that combines adaptive time-frequency analysis w
papers.summary: EssTFNet is a new deep learning framework that combines adaptive time-frequency analysis with DNA language models to predict essential genes in humans. The model is designed to be interpretable, helping researchers understand which sequence features contribute to gene essentiality. This approach could aid in identifying therapeutic targets and understanding gene function.

papers.title: DNA-DETR: sequence representation matters in object detection for functional genomic elements.
papers.abstract: Object detection has revolutionized multiple domains by enabling models to jointly classify and localize targets within data. Yet, its potential in genomic sequence analysis remains largely unexplored. Here, we introduce DNA-DETR, an adaptation of the DETR architecture for one-dimensional genomic object detection. Surprisingly, the direct application of object detection to DNA sequences yielded poor performance, even for elements with simple definitions such as Non-B DNA. We found that the widely used one-hot encoding failed to capture key structural features of several Non-B DNA types. To add
papers.summary: This paper introduces DNA-DETR, an adaptation of the DETR object detection architecture for identifying functional genomic elements in DNA sequences. The authors found that standard one-hot encoding fails to capture key structural features of elements like Non-B DNA, and they propose improved sequence representations to address this limitation.

papers.title: Automated tracking of the brown algal parasite Eurychasma dicksonii with deep learning.
papers.abstract: This study presents an automated real-time tracking approach for Eurychasma dicksonii, an intracellular parasite affecting brown algae, using deep learning applied to time-lapse microscopy video analysis. Traditionally, parasite detection and tracking rely on manual observation, resulting in a time-consuming, operator-dependent process prone to inaccuracies. To overcome these limitations, we developed and evaluated a comparative deep learning framework for parasite segmentation and multi-object tracking in microscopy videos. Specifically, we assessed five segmentation architectures, including
papers.summary: This study developed an automated deep learning framework to track the brown algal parasite Eurychasma dicksonii in time-lapse microscopy videos, replacing slow and error-prone manual observation. By comparing five segmentation architectures, the approach enables real-time, accurate detection and multi-object tracking of this intracellular parasite.

papers.title: ProMeta: a meta-learning framework for robust disease diagnosis and prediction from plasma proteomics.
papers.abstract: The plasma proteome offers a dynamic window of human health, capturing the real-time intersections between genetics and physiology. However, the application of deep learning to proteomics is currently hindered by a reliance on large-scale labeled datasets, rendering standard models ineffective for rare or novel diseases where patient samples are inherently scarce.
papers.summary: ProMeta is a new meta-learning framework designed to make deep learning models for plasma proteomics more effective even when labeled patient data is scarce, such as for rare or novel diseases. By learning from related tasks, it enables robust disease diagnosis and prediction without requiring large-scale labeled datasets.

papers.title: A novel transformer model of protein domains for viral taxonomy classification.
papers.abstract: Viruses with carefully curated taxonomic assignments (such as those in the ICTV taxonomy) still represent only a small fraction of viruses identified through sequencing data from virome or microbiome projects. It is therefore critical to develop methods that can assign viruses at multiple taxonomic ranks, so that a virus deemed novel at a given rank may still be placed into a higher-level taxon. Sequence-similarity-based approaches can classify viruses that share substantial genomic similarity with known viruses (e.g. those belonging to the same species or genus); however, their performance dr
papers.summary: This paper presents a novel transformer model that uses protein domain information to classify viruses into taxonomic ranks, even when they are novel at lower levels. The approach outperforms sequence-similarity-based methods for higher-level taxonomic assignments, addressing a key gap in viral classification from metagenomic data.

papers.title: USP-ddG: a unified structural paradigm with data efficacy and mixture-of-experts for predicting mutational effects on protein-protein interactions.
papers.abstract: Accurately estimating changes in binding free energy (ΔΔG) is critical for understanding protein-protein interactions (PPIs) and guiding rational protein design. Recent deep learning methods have achieved notable progress by pre-training on large-scale structural data. While some approaches explore structural flexibility through energy-based sampling or generative modeling, these strategies typically involve substantial computational cost and overlook data efficacy in terms of training data organization.
papers.summary: USP-ddG is a new deep learning method that predicts how mutations affect protein-protein binding strength (ΔΔG) without expensive structural sampling, using a mixture-of-experts approach to efficiently learn from limited training data. It achieves high accuracy while being computationally efficient, making it practical for guiding protein design.

papers.title: NPBIP: predicting binding preferences of uncharacterized nucleic-acid-binding proteins.
papers.abstract: Nucleic-acid-binding proteins (NBPs) are crucial regulators of gene expression, recognizing specific RNA or DNA binding sites. While high-throughput experiments have generated vast amounts of binding data, a significant challenge remains in predicting binding affinities of a novel query NBP to any nucleic-acid sequence. Current computational methods often require prior experimental data for the query NBP or are limited to predictions over predefined short RNA or DNA sequences.
papers.summary: This paper presents NPBIP, a new computational method that can predict the binding preferences of any nucleic-acid-binding protein (NBP) to any RNA or DNA sequence, even for proteins with no prior experimental data. Unlike existing tools that require prior data or are limited to short sequences, NPBIP uses a generalizable approach to predict binding affinities for uncharacterized NBPs. This could accelerate the study of gene regulation by enabling predictions for the many NBPs that lack experimental binding data.

papers.title: PU-GRAIL: residue-level graph learning for identifying protective bacterial antigens under positive-unlabeled supervision.
papers.abstract: The identification of protective antigens is fundamentally constrained by sparse annotations and pervasive label uncertainty in reverse vaccinology. Protective antigen-antibody interactions are mediated by a limited subset of surface-accessible residues that form spatially coherent epitopes. This motivates modeling antigenicity at the residue level, where 3D structure provides critical context for functional immune recognition. Moreover, antigen datasets are inherently positive unlabeled: unannotated proteins may contain hidden positives, making reliable negatives difficult to obtain.
papers.summary: PU-GRAIL is a new machine learning method that predicts protective bacterial antigens by analyzing protein structures at the residue level, rather than the whole protein level. It uses a graph neural network to model how surface residues form epitopes and handles the common problem of unlabeled data (where some unannotated proteins are actually protective antigens) using positive-unlabeled learning. This approach improves the identification of vaccine targets by leveraging 3D structural context and addressing label uncertainty.

papers.title: OrgNet+: towards robust protein stability prediction with convolutional neural networks.
papers.abstract: Predicting the effect of single-point mutations on protein stability is a central problem in molecular biology and protein engineering. Recent structure-based deep learning methods, particularly 3D convolutional neural networks (3D CNNs), have achieved strong predictive performance by leveraging high-resolution protein structures. However, proteins exist as heterogeneous conformational ensembles rather than single static structures, and the impact of conformational flexibility on structure-based ΔΔG predictors remains poorly characterized. Consequently, current models may yield unstable or eve
papers.summary: This paper introduces OrgNet+, a 3D convolutional neural network that improves protein stability prediction by accounting for conformational flexibility, addressing a key limitation of existing structure-based methods that rely on single static structures. The model demonstrates more robust predictions across conformational ensembles, highlighting the importance of considering protein dynamics for accurate ΔΔG estimation.

papers.title: seq2ribo: structure-aware integration of machine learning and simulation to predict ribosome location profiles from RNA sequences.
papers.abstract: Ribosome dynamics are vital in the process of protein expression. Current methods rely on ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq), RNA-seq profiles, and full genomic context. This restricts their use in de novo sequence design, like messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccines. Simulation-only approaches like the Totally Asymmetric Simple Exclusion Process (TASEP) oversimplify translation by focusing solely on codon elongation times.
papers.summary: This paper presents seq2ribo, a machine learning method that predicts ribosome location profiles from RNA sequences alone, without requiring experimental data like Ribo-seq or full genomic context. This enables de novo sequence design for applications like mRNA vaccines, overcoming limitations of simulation-only approaches that oversimplify translation dynamics.

papers.title: Agentomics: an agentic system that autonomously develops novel state-of-the-art solutions for biomedical machine learning tasks.
papers.abstract: Extracting knowledge from biomedical data is crucial for advancing our understanding of biological systems and developing novel therapeutics. The quantity, quality, and resolution of biomedical data constantly evolves, requiring the automation of biomedical machine learning (ML). Existing Automated ML tools lack flexibility, while large language models (LLMs) struggle to consistently deliver reproducible machine learning codebases, and existing LLM Agent-powered solutions lag behind human-engineered ML models.
papers.summary: Agentomics is a new AI agent system that can autonomously design and implement state-of-the-art machine learning solutions for biomedical data analysis, outperforming both existing automated ML tools and human-engineered models. It leverages large language models to flexibly generate reproducible codebases tailored to evolving biomedical datasets.

papers.title: MiRformer: a dual-transformer-encoder framework for predicting microRNA-mRNA interactions from paired sequences.
papers.abstract: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) regulate gene expression by binding to target messenger RNAs (mRNAs), inducing translational repression or mRNA degradation. Accurate prediction of miRNA-mRNA interactions and precise localization of binding and cleavage sites are critical for understanding post-transcriptional regulation and for enabling RNA therapeutics. Existing computational methods often rely on handcrafted or indirect features, scale poorly to kilobase-long mRNA sequences, or provide limited interpretability.
papers.summary: MiRformer is a new deep learning model that uses a dual-transformer-encoder architecture to predict which microRNAs bind to which messenger RNAs directly from their sequence data, without requiring handcrafted features. It can handle long mRNA sequences and provides interpretable predictions of binding and cleavage sites, improving upon existing methods for understanding gene regulation and designing RNA-based therapies.

papers.title: eGoT: enhanced graph-of-thoughts for multi-hop knowledge retrieval and hypothesis generation in biomedicine.
papers.abstract: Research on biological mechanisms and disease processes is limited by fragmented findings across unstructured text in publications. Question answering and hypothesis generation that can reason across multiple sources can overcome this limitation. However, Large language models (LLMs) are prone to inaccuracies and lack clear provenance to primary evidence. Retrieval augmented generation approaches that have provenance to the original source of evidence address these shortcomings. However, the response richness is dependent on the retrieval process design. Current approaches often fail to produc
papers.summary: This paper introduces eGoT, a method that enhances graph-of-thoughts reasoning for multi-hop knowledge retrieval and hypothesis generation in biomedicine. It addresses limitations of large language models by improving retrieval-augmented generation with better provenance and richer responses from fragmented biomedical literature.

papers.title: Deep learning-based dental age classification from panoramic radiographs: a multi-granularity, multi-model comparative study.
papers.abstract: To evaluate the performance of a hybrid deep learning architecture, MaxViT-T, for fine-grained dental age estimation (DAE) and coarse-grained age-group classification using panoramic radiographs, and to interpret the model's decision-making process through Explainable AI (XAI).
papers.summary: This study evaluates a hybrid deep learning model (MaxViT-T) for estimating dental age and classifying age groups from panoramic dental X-rays, showing it can perform both fine-grained and coarse-grained age classification. The model's decisions are interpreted using Explainable AI (XAI) to understand which features it uses. The approach could improve automated age estimation in forensic and clinical settings.

papers.title: Human gut flagellome profiling using FlaPro reveals TLR5-related phenotype-specific alterations in IBD.
papers.abstract: Flagellin, the structural protein of bacterial flagella, activates the innate immune receptor Toll-like receptor 5 (TLR5). However, the ability of different flagellins to bind and stimulate TLR5 varies widely, suggesting that the composition of an individual's flagellin repertoire, defined as flagellome, may influence host-microbiome interactions and inflammation. Here, we developed FlaPro, a computational pipeline for quantification and functional annotation of human gut flagellomes. Functional categories in FlaPro are derived from a machine learning model trained on experimentally characteri
papers.summary: The authors developed FlaPro, a computational pipeline that quantifies and functionally annotates the collection of flagellin proteins (the 'flagellome') in the human gut microbiome. Using this tool, they found that the composition of flagellins, which activate the immune receptor TLR5, is altered in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) patients and correlates with specific disease phenotypes.

papers.title: Predicting antibody self-association with sequence-structure fusion models: the central role of CSI-BLI in early developability screening.
papers.abstract: Antibody-based biologics are expanding rapidly, yet challenges in development from self-association, high viscosity, aggregation, and unfavorable clearance underscore the need for accurate in silico screening. Clone self-interaction biolayer interferometry (CSI-BLI) is a plate-based, low-material assay of weak, reversible self-association that serves as an early proxy for high-concentration viscosity and a complementary predictor of in vivo clearance. In a panel of 246 monoclonal antibodies, CSI-BLI moderately correlates with viscosity; further, in hFcRn Tg32 mice (41 antibodies), CSI-BLI stro
papers.summary: This paper presents a computational model that combines antibody sequence and structural features to predict self-association, a key factor in developability. The model is validated against CSI-BLI assay data from 246 antibodies and shows that CSI-BLI is a useful early proxy for viscosity and in vivo clearance. The approach offers a practical in silico screening tool for early antibody development.

papers.title: Growth factor thermostability dataset derived from experimental and computational approaches to accelerate cultured meat development.
papers.abstract: This project compiled experimentally measured and computationally predicted thermostability data for growth factors (GFs) commonly used in cultured meat and seafood (CM) production. Data were generated on recombinant GFs using thermal shift assays and combined with publicly available data using a structured literature curation workflow developed using a Large Language Model (LLM) with manual validation. In addition, in silico protein stability calculations were generated using FoldX. Data such as GF identity, species origin, recombinant host, variant information, method of measurement, and exp
papers.summary: This paper presents a dataset of thermostability measurements for growth factors used in cultured meat production, combining experimental thermal shift assays with computational predictions from FoldX and literature mining using a large language model. The dataset includes information on growth factor identity, species origin, and measurement methods, providing a resource for optimizing growth factor stability in cell culture media.

papers.title: Growth-rate analysis of clathrin assembly: inferring endocytic kinetics in living cells and tissues.
papers.abstract: Measuring protein kinetics in living cells remains challenging due to the complexity of intracellular environments and the limitations of live-cell imaging. Clathrin-mediated endocytosis (CME) provides a model system in which protein assembly dynamics encode functional outcomes, yet conventional approaches rely on reconstructing full trajectories of individual events, making them sensitive to tracking errors and difficult to extend to dense or in vivo systems. Here, we review growth rate-based analysis of clathrin assembly as an alternative framework for quantifying endocytic kinetics. By extr
papers.summary: This review presents growth-rate analysis as an alternative method for quantifying clathrin-mediated endocytosis kinetics, which avoids the tracking errors and limitations of traditional trajectory-based approaches. The method is particularly promising for studying protein dynamics in dense or in vivo systems where conventional live-cell imaging struggles.

papers.title: AI-Powered Discovery of Rosmarinic Acid as a Novel Ferroptosis Inhibitor for Ulcerative Colitis via Targeting the ALOX15-VDAC1 Axis.
papers.abstract: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease with limited mechanism-based therapies, and ferroptosis-an iron-dependent lipid peroxidation-driven cell death-has emerged as a critical pathogenic event. Using a machine learning model that combines molecular fingerprints and transfer learning representations to screen for ALOX15 inhibitors, we identified rosmarinic acid (RA) from natural product libraries. RA exhibited high-affinity binding to ALOX15 and maintained stable complex conformation in molecular dynamics simulations. In dextran sulfate sodium-induced colitis mice and l
papers.summary: This study used a machine learning model combining molecular fingerprints and transfer learning to screen for inhibitors of ALOX15, identifying rosmarinic acid (RA) as a novel ferroptosis inhibitor. RA showed high-affinity binding to ALOX15 and effectively treated ulcerative colitis in a mouse model by targeting the ALOX15-VDAC1 axis, offering a potential new therapeutic approach.

papers.title: Machine learning-based prediction of difficult laryngoscopy in infants with Pierre Robin sequence using quantitative 3D computed tomography parameters.
papers.abstract: Infants with Pierre Robin sequence (PRS) frequently present with difficult laryngoscopic exposure due to mandibular hypoplasia, glossoptosis, and upper airway narrowing. Quantitative three-dimensional computed tomography (3D-CT) enables objective characterization of airway anatomy and may improve preoperative airway assessment. This study aimed to identify key 3D-CT parameters associated with difficult laryngoscopic exposure in infants with PRS and to develop and externally validate machine learning-based predictive models.
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning to predict difficult laryngoscopy in infants with Pierre Robin sequence by analyzing quantitative 3D CT scans of the airway. The models identify key anatomical parameters that can help clinicians anticipate airway challenges before surgery, potentially improving preoperative planning and safety.

papers.title: Discovery of hub genes linking oxidative stress to type 2 diabetic sarcopenia using single-cell sequencing and machine learning.
papers.abstract: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) and sarcopenia demonstrate a significant comorbidity, particularly in the elderly, yet the molecular mechanisms linking them, especially through oxidative stress, remain incompletely understood. This study aimed to identify oxidative stress-related hub genes involved in T2DM-associated sarcopenia (T2DS) by integrating single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and bulk RNA-seq data with machine learning. We analyzed scRNA-seq datasets (GSE244515, GSE268953) to characterize cellular heterogeneity and bulk RNA-seq datasets (GSE202295, GSE226151) for differential expre
papers.summary: This study identifies key genes linking oxidative stress to sarcopenia in type 2 diabetes by combining single-cell and bulk RNA sequencing with machine learning. The findings highlight potential molecular targets for understanding and treating muscle wasting in diabetic patients.

papers.title: SST subseasonal-to-seasonal forecasting: A heterogeneous three-path fusion model with wavelet decomposition.
papers.abstract: El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO), the Earth's dominant mode of interannual climate variability, is closely linked to sea surface temperature (SST) variability in the central and eastern equatorial Pacific. Accurate subseasonal-to-seasonal SST forecasting in this region supports ocean monitoring and environmental assessment. We propose WDFormer (Wave Decomposition Former), a daily SST forecasting model built on a "decomposition, multi-path processing, and adaptive fusion" paradigm. The model uses 365 daily SST observations from local 4×4 grids around representative ENSO monitoring stations t
papers.summary: This paper presents WDFormer, a deep learning model that improves subseasonal-to-seasonal sea surface temperature (SST) forecasting by combining wavelet decomposition with a three-path fusion architecture. The model processes daily SST observations from local grid regions around ENSO monitoring stations to enhance prediction accuracy for climate variability. This work offers a methodological advance for environmental monitoring and climate forecasting applications.
